mnemonics.title: Deque Operations
mnemonics.content: Front and Rear both Insert and Delete - think FRee Deque: Front Remove, Rear Entry... actually FRID (Front Rear Insert Delete) - all 4 operations!

mnemonics.title: Input vs Output Restricted
mnemonics.content: Input Restricted = Insert Restricted (only one end accepts new). Output Restricted = Delete Restricted (only one end removes). Think: I-Insert, O-Output(Delete).

mnemonics.title: Circular Array Capacity
mnemonics.content: For n-size array, store n-1 elements max. Remember: 'One slot lost to distinguish full from empty' - FULL = (R+1)%N == F.

acronyms.usage: Both ends support enqueue and dequeue operations
Insert at one end only, delete from both ends
Insert at both ends, delete from one end only

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a clock face. Front and rear are hands that move clockwise. When front 'chases' rear, deque is full. The empty slot between them when full represents the distinguishing condition.

Think of a railway siding with two ends. insertFront adds to left, insertRear adds to right. deleteFront removes from left, deleteRear removes from right. Circular array is like an oval track where trains circle back.

analogies.analogy: A railway siding (dead-end track) where trains can enter from either end but only one train can pass through at a time - exactly like a deque with two operational ends.
Like two people reading a word from opposite ends towards the center, comparing characters - the deque mirrors this by holding characters from both ends.
Imagine two side-by-side notebooks: one for actions done (redo stack), one for actions undone (undo stack). Adding an action clears the redo notebook. This is exactly how deques implement undo-redo.
